md: Multiplying and dividing positive whole numbers is familiar territory. Negatives change one thing — the **sign** of the result. The numbers themselves still work the same way.
**Same signs → positive.** Multiplying or dividing two numbers with the same sign always gives a positive result.
**Different signs → negative.** Multiplying or dividing numbers with different signs always gives a negative result.

blocks.title: Why two negatives make a positive
blocks.body: Multiplication is repeated addition. $3 \times 4 = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$. So $-3 \times 4 = -(3 \times 4) = -12$ (taking three fours, then negating). And $-3 \times -4 = -(3 \times -4) = -(-12) = 12$. Negation flips signs; flip twice and you're back to where you started.

md: Same rules — division and multiplication share the sign rules. $\dfrac{12}{-4} = -3$. $\dfrac{-20}{-5} = 4$.
When you multiply more than two numbers, just count the negatives.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Same signs → positive. Different signs → negative.** That's the whole rule, for multiplication and division both. For longer products, count negatives — even → positive, odd → negative.
